data.title: Cyngn Enters Agriculture With Chandler Automation Alliance
data.description:
Cyngn Inc. (NASDAQ:CYN) stock rose Tuesday after it revealed a new dealer partnership in the agriculture sector.

The autonomous industrial vehicle firm announced the agreement with Chandler Automation to push its DriveMod Tugger into food processing and packing operations.

The collaboration adds Chandler Automation to Cyngn's dealer network to sell the DriveMod Tugger.

Also Read: Stock Market Today: Nasdaq, S&P 500 Futures Rise As Street Awaits Q3 GDP Report—Parsons, ZIM, Trump Media & Technology In Focus

The autonomous tugger can move materials repeatedly between processing and packaging stations.

Food processors and packers aim to cut labor needs and improve throughput with automation.

Chandler Automation builds factory automation systems, including optical sorting, inspection, robotic palletizing, and custom machinery. The company said its tailored systems help food processors lower labor costs and boost efficiency.

Chandler's position in the agriculture ecosystem gives Cyngn a direct path into the sector.

Cyngn's Strategic Rationale

Cyngn has expanded DriveMod deployments across multiple industries and now targets agriculture as the next growth area.

The DriveMod platform brings autonomous movement to existing industrial vehicles without major infrastructure changes.

Cyngn said the new dealer relationship broadens its reach and adds a new customer base.

"Chandler Automation is deeply embedded in the food processing ecosystem," said Lior Tal, CEO of Cyngn.

He said autonomous material movement can increase uptime and throughput for food producers.

"Many of our customers have modernized inspection and packaging but still rely on manual transport between stations," said Sean Chandler, CEO of Chandler Automation.

He said Cyngn's technology fits the continuous-flow operations his customers want.

CYN Price Action: Cyngn shares were up 0.95% at $3.20 during premarket trading on Tuesday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

  • Dan Ives Calls Microsoft ‘Rodney Dangerfield' Of AI — Investors Are Giving It ‘No Respect'

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: FDA Asks Reviva Pharmaceuticals To Conduct Additional Phase 3 Study For Its Schizophrenia Drug
data.description:
Reviva Pharmaceuticals Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:RVPH) on Tuesday announced a regulatory update following a pre-New Drug Application (pre-NDA) meeting with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA).

The update is regarding brilaroxazine, a novel serotonin-dopamine and neuroinflammatory signaling modulator, in late-stage development for schizophrenia.

In written feedback, the FDA recommended a second Phase 3 clinical trial for brilaroxazine in patients with schizophrenia to, among other things, generate additional efficacy data and expand the safety dataset.

Subject to sufficient financing, Reviva plans to initiate the RECOVER-2 Phase 3 trial in the first half of 2026.

The RECOVER-2 trial will be similar in design to the completed RECOVER Phase 3 trial of brilaroxazine.

Also Read: Bristol Myers Pushes Data Readout For Key Alzheimer’s Psychosis Study After Irregularities

“We appreciate the clear and constructive feedback from the FDA…, with data reflecting broad-spectrum efficacy, a well-characterized and generally favorable safety profile, and favorable treatment adherence observed to date, with convenient once-daily oral administration,” said Laxminarayan Bhat, Founder, President, and CEO of Reviva.

The recommendation follows FDA review of the company’s existing nonclinical and clinical data package, including two completed clinical trials (one Phase 2 trial and one Phase 3 trial that included a 1-year open-label extension) and clinical pharmacology studies.

The company said that brilaroxazine has demonstrated:

Broad-spectrum efficacy in major symptom domains of schizophrenia, including negative symptoms, in the 790 subjects that participated in the Phase 2 and Phase 3 clinical trials.

A generally well-tolerated safety profile, observed in over 900 subjects treated to date.

On Tuesday, China’s National Medical Products Administration (NMPA) approved the New Drug Application (NDA) for Cobenfy (xanomeline and trospium chloride) for the treatment of schizophrenia in adults.

Zai Lab Limited (NASDAQ:ZLAB) has an exclusive license from Karuna Therapeutics, Inc., a company acquired by Bristol Myers Squibb Co (NYSE:BMY), to develop, manufacture, and commercialize Cobenfy in Greater China (mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau, and Taiwan, collectively).

The approval is supported by the results from a Phase 1 pharmacokinetics study conducted in China, the Phase 3 China study (ZL-2701-001), and data from three global EMERGENT clinical studies.

RVPH Price Action: Reviva Pharmaceuticals shares were down 48.54% at $0.30 during premarket trading on Monday. The stock is trading near its 52-week low of $0.25, according to Benzinga Pro data.

  • TikTok Parent ByteDance To Invest $23 Billion In AI To Compete With US Tech Giants: Report

Photo by Kateryna Kon via Shutterstock

data.title: Here's How Much $1000 Invested In Alcoa 5 Years Ago Would Be Worth Today
data.description:
Alcoa (NYSE:AA) has outperformed the market over the past 5 years by 6.32% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 19.29%. Currently, Alcoa has a market capitalization of $13.97 billion.

data.title: AECOM Lands Role In Multi-Billion-Dollar Scotland Water Overhaul
data.description:
AECOM (NYSE:ACM) disclosed on Tuesday that it has been named a preferred bidder for Scottish Water's Enterprise Alliance.

Enterprise Alliance is a multi-billion-dollar program lasting up to 13 years to upgrade Scotland's water and wastewater infrastructure.

The Enterprise is Scottish Water's most significant investment to date.

Notably, AECOM has been selected as one of two Primary Designers for the Enterprise Alliance, responsible for design across the program and ensuring efficient delivery.

The initiative runs from 2027 to 2033, with work starting in 2026 and the option to extend the program by up to six additional years.

Richard Whitehead, chief executive of AECOM's Europe and India region, said the company's enterprise-style delivery model "sets a new benchmark for the industry," enabling closer collaboration and greater efficiency, while leveraging AECOM's technical depth and engineering-led AI capabilities to improve certainty on complex capital programs.

Recent Earnings

In November, the company reported fourth-quarter revenue growth of 2% year over year (Y/Y) to $4.175 billion, missing the consensus of $4.315 billion. Adjusted EPS of $1.36 (+7% Y/Y) beat the consensus of $1.34.

AECOM expects fiscal 2026 adjusted EPS of $5.65 and $5.85 (versus consensus of $5.24), adjusted EBITDA of $1.265 billion-$1.305 billion, and free cash flow of around $400 million.

ACM Price Action: AECOM shares edged higher Monday, closing up 0.89% at $97.58, according to Benzinga Pro data.

data.title: What Factors Are Transforming Bahrain's Economy?
data.description:
Bahrain, one of the smallest nations in the Gulf Cooperation Council, is quietly but decisively reshaping its economic future. Long known for its oil legacy and role as a regional financial hub, the island kingdom is now navigating a broader transformation driven by diversification, infrastructure investment, regulatory reform, and a forward-looking national vision. At a time when Gulf economies are rethinking growth models, Bahrain's approach offers a compelling case study in strategic adaptation.

Transitioning Away from Oil Dependence

For decades, oil and gas revenues underpinned Bahrain's public finances. Before the pandemic, hydrocarbons accounted for roughly 81 percent of government revenue. By 2022, that figure had declined to around 62.6 percent, reflecting deliberate policy choices to expand non-oil income sources. Measures such as the introduction of a 10 percent value-added tax and broader fiscal reforms have helped stabilize revenues while reducing exposure to energy price volatility.

This transition is not about abandoning oil overnight, but about building resilience. Bahrain's leadership has recognized that long-term stability depends on cultivating sectors that can generate sustainable growth, employment, and foreign investment beyond hydrocarbons.

Financial Services as a Cornerstone of Growth

Bahrain's financial sector remains one of its strongest assets. As one of the region's earliest banking centers, the country has leveraged its regulatory maturity to stay competitive. In the first half of 2024, financial services grew by 3.6 percent, supported by proactive policies from the Central Bank of Bahrain.

The regulator has positioned the country as a testing ground for innovation, embracing fintech sandboxes, open banking frameworks, and the regulation of digital assets and crypto-related services. This has attracted regional startups as well as international firms looking for a stable yet flexible jurisdiction. Finance, once reliant on traditional banking, is now evolving into a broader ecosystem that supports technology, payments, and digital infrastructure.

Tourism and Hospitality Take Center Stage

Tourism has emerged as one of the most visible drivers of Bahrain's economic transformation. The government has set an ambitious target of attracting 14.1 million visitors by 2026, with tourism's contribution to GDP expected to reach 11.4 percent. These goals are underpinned by heavy investment in both infrastructure and destination development.

Luxury resorts, mixed-use waterfront projects, and resort cities are reshaping the country's tourism landscape. Developments such as Al Sahel Resort, Hasabi, Durrat Dawn, and Durrat Crescent are designed to appeal to high-value visitors while expanding Bahrain's offerings beyond short stays and business travel.

A critical enabler of this strategy is the modernization of Bahrain International Airport. The $1.1 billion upgrade increases annual passenger capacity from 9 million to 14 million, reinforcing Bahrain's role as a regional gateway and improving connectivity for both tourism and commerce.

Manufacturing and Industrial Development

While smaller in scale than its GCC peers, Bahrain's manufacturing sector plays a strategic role in diversification. The country has focused on attracting investments in higher-value industries such as technology-driven manufacturing, logistics, and downstream industrial activities.

Favorable regulations, access to regional markets, and established industrial zones have supported this growth. Strategic partnerships with international firms are helping Bahrain move up the value chain, positioning manufacturing not as a volume-driven sector, but as a contributor to productivity and export competitiveness.

The Current Economic Environment

In 2024, Bahrain's economy is projected to grow by around 3.5 percent, with non-oil sectors leading the expansion. Inflation is expected to remain subdued at approximately 1.0 percent, providing a relatively stable cost environment for businesses and consumers.

Fiscal pressures persist, with a projected budget deficit of around 3.1 percent of GDP, reflecting lower oil revenues and ongoing public investment. Higher global interest rates have moderated private consumption and real estate activity, but policymakers appear focused on balancing short-term constraints with long-term reform priorities.

Vision 2030 and the Road Ahead

Bahrain's Vision 2030 provides the strategic framework guiding these changes. At its core is economic diversification supported by digital transformation. The information and communications technology market is expected to grow at a compound annual rate of 6.7 percent by 2027, with significant revenue opportunities emerging between 2022 and 2026.

Sustainability is another central pillar. Bahrain faces environmental challenges, including water scarcity and pressure on marine ecosystems. In response, the government is promoting green technologies, efficient resource management, and sustainable urban planning to ensure growth does not come at the expense of environmental resilience.

Labor market reform remains a work in progress. While progress has been made, issues such as youth unemployment and skills mismatches persist. Vision 2030 emphasizes job creation, workforce upskilling, and closer alignment between education and market needs to ensure Bahrainis are equipped to participate in emerging sectors.

Infrastructure and Strategic Investment

Infrastructure development is reinforcing these ambitions. Bahrain's Strategic Projects Plan outlines over $30 billion in investments across transport, logistics, real estate, and industrial infrastructure. These projects are designed to enhance national connectivity, attract private investment, and support priority sectors critical to diversification.

Equally important is the emphasis on fairness and social equity. Vision 2030 stresses inclusive growth, transparent governance, consumer protection, and equal access to education and healthcare. Social safety nets and targeted job training programs aim to ensure that economic transformation delivers broad-based benefits.

Conclusion

Bahrain's economic transformation is shaped by a combination of necessity and strategy. Faced with structural limits to oil-driven growth, the country has chosen diversification, regulatory reform, and targeted investment as its path forward. Tourism, finance, manufacturing, and digital services are redefining the economic landscape, while infrastructure upgrades and social reforms aim to sustain momentum.

As Bahrain moves toward its Vision 2030 goals, the challenge will be maintaining fiscal discipline while continuing to invest in future-ready sectors. If current trends hold, the kingdom's measured, reform-driven approach may well position it as one of the more adaptable and resilient economies in the Gulf.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.
